critic_raw:
This hyperspectral crop yield prediction experiment is more carefully designed than many, but retains several methodological problems.

First, the split strategy is internally contradictory. The design describes a 'spatio-temporal stratified split' but then describes the mechanism as 'randomly sampling field-seasons while maintaining proportions.' A true spatio-temporal split would hold out entire field-seasons—all images from Season 3 across all fields—ensuring the model is tested on entirely unseen time periods and geographic regions. Instead, the described random sampling across field-seasons allows images from the same field in the same season to appear in both training and test sets (if the unit of randomization is images rather than field-seasons). This ambiguity must be resolved: if the unit is field-season pairs, it is defensible; if it is individual images, there is spatial leakage.

Second, the design includes field ID as a categorical feature (as 'fixed effects or embeddings'). If the same field appears in training and test data (which the stratified random approach makes possible), the model learns field-specific intercepts that do not generalize to new fields. In deployment, the model will encounter fields not seen in training; field ID embeddings trained on known fields provide no predictive signal and may degrade performance on novel fields.

Third, the CNN operates on 'hyperspectral image patches' but the spatial structure of patches relative to yield labels is underspecified. If yield labels are at the field-season level but patches are at sub-field resolution, there is a units-of-analysis mismatch: multiple patches from the same field share the same label, inflating effective sample size and creating pseudo-replication.

Fourth, the design does not address spatial autocorrelation in residuals. Adjacent fields or images within the same region will have correlated yield outcomes (shared weather, soil type, irrigation). Bootstrap confidence intervals on RMSE that treat individual image predictions as independent will be too narrow, understating uncertainty.

Fifth, the comparison between CNN operating on raw spectral patches and MLR operating on PCA-reduced or band-selected features is not architecturally equivalent. The CNN processes spatial context through convolutions, while MLR receives flattened spectral features without spatial context. The design does not control for whether spatial processing (not just CNN's representational power) drives performance differences.
all_issues_raised: Split strategy is ambiguous: if randomization is at the image level rather than field-season pair level, spatial leakage occurs as images from the same field appear in both train and test.
Field ID as a feature creates spurious in-sample generalization; deployed on new fields, the embedding provides no signal and the model's real-world performance will be worse than test-set estimates.
Units-of-analysis mismatch: multiple patches per field share a single yield label, inflating effective sample size and creating pseudo-replication that biases RMSE estimates.
Spatial autocorrelation in yield outcomes is not modeled; bootstrap CIs on RMSE treat patch predictions as independent, producing unrealistically narrow uncertainty estimates.

Comparison is not architecturally controlled: CNN processes spatial context via convolutions while MLR receives flattened spectral features; performance difference may reflect spatial processing rather than representational capacity.

round_transcripts.critic:
This hyperspectral crop yield prediction experiment is more carefully designed than many, but retains several methodological problems.

First, the split strategy is internally contradictory. The design describes a 'spatio-temporal stratified split' but then describes the mechanism as 'randomly sampling field-seasons while maintaining proportions.' A true spatio-temporal split would hold out entire field-seasons—all images from Season 3 across all fields—ensuring the model is tested on entirely unseen time periods and geographic regions. Instead, the described random sampling across field-seasons allows images from the same field in the same season to appear in both training and test sets (if the unit of randomization is images rather than field-seasons). This ambiguity must be resolved: if the unit is field-season pairs, it is defensible; if it is individual images, there is spatial leakage.

Second, the design includes field ID as a categorical feature (as 'fixed effects or embeddings'). If the same field appears in training and test data (which the stratified random approach makes possible), the model learns field-specific intercepts that do not generalize to new fields. In deployment, the model will encounter fields not seen in training; field ID embeddings trained on known fields provide no predictive signal and may degrade performance on novel fields.

Third, the CNN operates on 'hyperspectral image patches' but the spatial structure of patches relative to yield labels is underspecified. If yield labels are at the field-season level but patches are at sub-field resolution, there is a units-of-analysis mismatch: multiple patches from the same field share the same label, inflating effective sample size and creating pseudo-replication.

Fourth, the design does not address spatial autocorrelation in residuals. Adjacent fields or images within the same region will have correlated yield outcomes (shared weather, soil type, irrigation). Bootstrap confidence intervals on RMSE that treat individual image predictions as independent will be too narrow, understating uncertainty.

Fifth, the comparison between CNN operating on raw spectral patches and MLR operating on PCA-reduced or band-selected features is not architecturally equivalent. The CNN processes spatial context through convolutions, while MLR receives flattened spectral features without spatial context. The design does not control for whether spatial processing (not just CNN's representational power) drives performance differences.
round_transcripts.defender:
The critique identifies real ambiguities, though their severity varies considerably.

On Issue 1 (split ambiguity): The design states 'randomly sampling field-seasons' as the unit, which suggests the randomization is at the field-season pair level—not individual images. If a field-season pair is assigned to test, all images from that field in that season go to test. This resolves the spatial leakage concern at the field-season level, though between-season leakage for the same field is still possible if Season 3 fields also appear in Season 1/2 training data. The design should be clarified to state explicitly whether fields can appear in multiple splits.

On Issue 2 (field ID embedding): This is a legitimate deployment concern. If the model is intended for generalization to novel fields, field ID should be excluded from the feature set or treated as a random effect estimated at inference time. The defender acknowledges that fixed field embeddings do not generalize to unseen fields.

On Issue 3 (pseudo-replication): This is a genuine concern if patches are the unit of prediction and yield labels are at field-season level. The correct approach is to aggregate patch predictions to field-season level before computing RMSE, so the evaluation metric reflects field-season accuracy rather than patch accuracy. If this aggregation is already intended but not stated, it should be made explicit.

On Issue 4 (spatial autocorrelation): Standard bootstrap treats observations as exchangeable, which is violated by spatial correlation. Spatial block bootstrap or cluster-robust standard errors are more appropriate. This is a real limitation of the proposed uncertainty quantification.

On Issue 5 (spatial processing): The CNN's convolutions do provide spatial context that MLR cannot access from flattened features. However, the comparison is framed as 'CNN vs. MLR for hyperspectral imagery,' and spatial feature extraction is inherent to the CNN approach. The comparison tests the full pipeline difference, which is the operationally relevant question.

round_transcripts.critic:
Remaining unresolved issues:

Issue 2 (field ID embedding): The defender concedes that fixed field embeddings do not generalize to unseen fields. If the test set contains fields that also appear in training (which the stratified random approach permits), test performance is artificially inflated by field-specific memorization. The experiment cannot claim to measure generalization to new fields—only interpolation accuracy within seen fields. This must be disclosed or the feature removed.

Issue 3 (pseudo-replication): If RMSE is computed at the patch level rather than the aggregated field-season level, the reported metric is not the ecologically valid unit. Pseudo-replication can make a model appear far more accurate than it is for the actual prediction task (field-level yield estimation). The evaluation protocol must specify the aggregation level.
round_transcripts.defender:
On Issue 2: Concurred. Field ID as a fixed-effect feature creates circularity for out-of-sample field generalization. The feature should be removed from the test-time feature set, or the study should explicitly scope claims to 'fields present in training data.' If the experiment is intended as proof-of-concept on known fields, the scope limitation must be prominently disclosed.

On Issue 3: Patch-level RMSE is not an appropriate primary metric if the operational goal is field-season yield estimation. The protocol should specify patch-to-field aggregation (e.g., mean predicted yield across patches within a field-season) and report RMSE at the field-season level. Without this, the reported performance numbers do not correspond to the stated objective.

all_issues_adjudicated: Field ID as a fixed-effect feature inflates test performance for known fields and does not generalize to unseen fields; claims must be scoped accordingly or the feature removed.
Units-of-analysis mismatch between patch-level predictions and field-season yield labels creates pseudo-replication; RMSE should be aggregated to field-season level before reporting.
